config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. Society matters, but I do not think it should automatically outrank individual rights, because that can easily become an excuse for abuse by governments or majorities. In my view, a fair society protects basic individual rights first, while still asking people to accept reasonable limits for public safety and the common good. From my perspective, the strongest societies are the ones that do not sacrifice human dignity just because it is convenient.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should matter, but I do not believe they should automatically take priority over individual rights. From a U.S. perspective, I am wary of any argument that lets the government or a majority override basic freedoms too easily, because history shows how quickly that can be abused. At the same time, individual rights are not absolute in every situation; public health, safety, and fair laws sometimes require real limits. The right balance is protecting core liberties first while allowing narrow, justified restrictions when society truly needs them.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should matter, but they should not automatically take priority over the rights of the individual. From an Indonesian perspective, I am very aware that appeals to “the common good” can be used to silence dissent, excuse corruption, or pressure minorities, so I do not trust that logic when it is too broad. At the same time, I do think individual rights come with responsibilities, and in cases like public health, disaster response, or preventing violence, limited restrictions can be justified if they are clear and proportional. The key point is that society is strongest when it protects people, not when it treats individual rights as disposable.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not automatically take priority over the rights of the individual. From a U.S. perspective, I am especially cautious about letting “the common good” become a blank check for government power or majority rule, because that has been used to justify serious abuses in the past. That said, individual rights are not a license to ignore public health, safety, or basic fairness, so I do think limited and specific restrictions can be justified when they are clearly necessary. In my view, the burden should always be on society to prove why a right must be limited, not on the individual to prove why it deserves protection.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not automatically take priority over the rights of the individual. From my perspective in Indonesia, I have seen how easily “for the public good” can become a cover for silencing criticism, protecting power, or pressuring minorities, so I do not trust that phrase when it is too broad. At the same time, I do not defend selfish individualism; public health, disaster response, and safety can justify limited restrictions, but only if they are clear, proportional, and accountable. Society is healthier when it protects individual dignity first, because a community that can dispose of rights too easily is not truly strong.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should matter, but they should not outrank individual rights by default. From a United States perspective, I am especially suspicious of broad claims about the "common good" because they can be used to justify censorship, discrimination, or government overreach, and our history gives plenty of reasons to be cautious. That said, I do not believe individual rights are unlimited in every case; public health, safety, and fair laws can justify narrow restrictions when the need is real and the limits are proportionate. To me, the burden should always be on society to prove why a right must be restricted, not on the person to prove why it deserves protection.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
